lines: Overview
During a typical block-level implementation flow, post-route setup and/or hold timing
optimization occurs followed by a full re-check of timing. For a variety of reasons discussed in
this app note, the final timing reported by optDesign -postRoute may not exactly match
timeDesign -postRoute. We’ll discuss:
• Known areas of potential problems and acceptable degree of differences
• A process to go through possible causes
• Ways to check whether these causes are affecting your runs
• Methods for minimizing potential differences
Nature of the Problem
When optDesign finishes, it reports timing to the console/log and writes detailed path reports to a
directory (the default is “timingReports”). Still, in a typical flow, there are valid reasons and
scenarios where a subsequent call to timeDesign (or other commands which call for a timing
snapshot) to occur:
• After optDesign finishes an interactive session is used to triage failing paths
• A user is interested in breaking out detailed path reports for each view so timeDesign is
called with the -expandedViews option
• The flow is broken up into multiple steps and exits/enters the tool between post-route
optimization and a final “signoff” stage
Depending on the scenario, timing may not precisely match between what optDesign says when
it finishes and what timeDesign reports.
COPYRIGHT © 2013, CADENCE DESIGN SYSTEMS, INC. PAGE 3
Here is an example of unacceptably large differences between timing reported by optDesign and
timeDesign.
When optDesign -postRoute finishes:
| Setup mode | all | reg2reg | in2reg | reg2out | in2out | clkgate |
| Violating Paths:| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
timeDesign -postRoute (in a subsequent session):
| Setup mode | all | reg2reg | in2reg | reg2out | in2out | clkgate |
| Violating Paths:| 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
In the reports above, note the following:
• The WNS in the in2reg group went from +0.023 to -0.052 (a degradation of 0.075ns)
which we consider an unacceptable amount of difference
• There are a small number of paths (6) affected relative to the total paths
• The reg2reg path group’s WNS differs by 4ps which is considered to be an acceptable
difference
Possible Causes
When unacceptably large timing differences arise here are some things that may be causing the
discrepancy:
• Different mode settings between the two sessions
• Timing correlation settings conveyed with encrypted TCL aren’t sourced in new session
• optDesign finished with an incremental TQRC extraction (and other incremental
extractions probably occurred during the optimization process) followed by timeDesign
with a full re-extraction
• Minor differences in extraction depending on the number of CPUs used for TQRC
COPYRIGHT © 2013, CADENCE DESIGN SYSTEMS, INC. PAGE 4
Mode Settings
If the timing differs when comparing optDesign -postRoute and timeDesign -postRoute between
two different Encounter sessions, the first thing to check is whether “mode” settings (like
setDelayCalMode, setSIMode, and setExtractRCMode) or timing globals may have inadvertently
changed.
To assess whether this might be the case compare the mode settings between the two runs as
follows:
encounter 1 > optDesign -postRoute
encounter 2 > saveDesign 1.enc
<start new session, restore design>
encounter 1> timeDesign -postRoute
encounter 2> saveDesign 2.enc
UNIX> diff 1.enc.dat/<design>.mode 2.enc.dat/<design>.mode
UNIX> diff 1.enc.dat/<design>.globals 2.enc.dat/<design>.globals
The mode files should contain only non-default settings. If there are differences between the
mode or globals files try setting the modes/globals from the session where optDesign was run
within the new session and re-timing the design. If the mode files are the same something else is
causing the difference.
Encrypted Timing Correlation Settings
If you happen to be using encrypted timing correlation settings, be sure to source them at the
beginning of the new session after restoring the design. Encrypted settings aren’t fully captured
in the .mode file so they’ll need to be re-asserted within each session they’re used.
Incr vs. Full TQRC
Within optDesign -postRoute and timeDesign -postRoute when TQRC is active
(setExtractRCMode -engine postRoute -effortLevel medium) the tool automatically determines
when and how to re-extract the design.
When optDesign or timeDesign is called, depending on whether the design has already been
extracted prior to calling the command, the tool will trigger an extraction of the design to
estimate parasitics. Within optDesign the tool will further elect how to re-extract the design to
update parasitics and timing. TQRC will extract the design one of these ways:
• Full: Used when the design isn’t in an extracted state when coming into
optDesign/timeDesign or when a step has sufficiently changed the design
• Incremental: Used when the design was previously extracted and “small” changes are
performed by an optimization step
COPYRIGHT © 2013, CADENCE DESIGN SYSTEMS, INC. PAGE 5
• None: If an optimization step within optDesign results in no changes to the design the
tool will skip extraction altogether
The tool uses Incremental TQRC to speed run-time when re-extracting the design after small
changes. This can introduce small differences in timing (usually less than 10ps). When larger
differences occur, steps later in this app note can help determine whether they’re caused
incremental vs. full TQRC.
Figures 1 and 2 below outline the steps involved in optDesign -postRoute (setup) and optDesign
-postRoute -hold. Extraction (full, incremental, or none) occurs at the beginning of optDesign
(and timeDesign) and after each optimization step once routing has been updated.

title: Log File Examples of Full/Incr/No TQRC Extraction

lines: Log File Examples of Full/Incr/No TQRC Extraction
Full:
Turbo QRC (TQRC) Extraction in Multi-Corner mode called for design 'xyz'.
Number of corners is 3.
No TQRC parasitic data in encounter. Going for fullchip extraction.
TQRC Extraction invoked in multi (8) processes mode on local host.
Incremental:
Region and/or Nets changed is small. Going for incremental extraction.
None:
No changed net or region found. No need to perform incremental extraction.
For a quick way to assess whether differences between incremental and full TQRC might be
coming into play, consult the log files for calls to incremental extraction, especially consecutive
ones during optDesign -postRoute (setup) and -hold.
COPYRIGHT © 2013, CADENCE DESIGN SYSTEMS, INC. PAGE 7

title: Triaging  Incremental vs. Full TQRC Differences

lines: Triaging Incremental vs. Full TQRC Differences
To determine whether differences in incremental vs. full TQRC are causing timing differences
use one of the following approaches:
• Use “saveDesign –rc/restoreDesign” to save the design with parasitic information
included. A timeDesign in a subsequent session would then use the same parasitic data as
when optDesign completed.
• Write out a SPEF after optDesign –postRoute and another after timeDesign –postRoute
(using “rcOut –rc_corner <rc_corner_name(s)> -spef <rc_corner>.spef.gz”) and compare
TCAP (total capacitance) and XCAP (cross coupling capacitance) using Ostrich.
If timing is still different it is likely caused by something else.

title: TQRC Differences Related to Multi-CPU Usage

lines: TQRC Differences Related to Multi-CPU Usage
Using MultiCPU can change the extraction results by a tiny amount. Ostrich comparisons of runs
with 8 vs. 1 CPU shows 1.001 and 0.999 recommended scale factors and these differences have
not been shown to cause timing differences. Nonetheless to eliminate MultiCPU non-
determinism as a source of timing differences suppress multiCPU with “setMultiCpuUsage -
localCpu 1”; obviously, this will mean an increase in runtime.
Workarounds Related to Incremental TQRC
If timing differences are problematically large and they’re confirmed to be due to differences in
incremental TQRC vs. full TQRC the next step is to examine the nature of the violating paths.
Look at a detailed path reports using “report_timing -path_type full_clock” after timeDesign for
the following possibilities:
• Presence of a large number of “weak” driver cells in critical/near-critical paths
• Delay differences in launch/capture clock instances
Avoiding Weak Driver Cells
Small differences in load capacitance or input slew can cause large differences in stage delay of
weak driver cells. Design operations that can cause these differences include:
• Routing updates during post route optimization which result in changes to the parasitic
network.
• Metal fill addition which can increase capacitive loading on nets even when fill is added
far away.
For these and other reasons, library providers discourage the use of weak cells in critical/near-
critical paths.
COPYRIGHT © 2013, CADENCE DESIGN SYSTEMS, INC. PAGE 8
To see if weak cells are in your critical paths, look at the detailed path report for the worst path
reported by timeDesign for the presence of weak cells. Using weak cells saves power but they’re
not recommended to be used on critical or near critical paths.
To prohibit the tool from swapping in weak cells altogether use: “setDontUse *D0* true” (for
example). This approach can be combined with a leakage power optimization target slack to
guardband against leakage power optimization creating problematic situations where near-
critical paths have weak cells. Example script snippet below:
setOptMode –leakagePowerEffort none
optDesign –postRoute
setDontUse *D0* true
setOptMode –setupTargetSlack 0.2
optLeakagePower
Disabling Incremental TQRC
If you’re convinced incremental vs. full TQRC is causing problematic timing differences in your
flow, and you can tolerate a ~20% run-time penalty during optDesign -postRoute, you can use
“setExtractRCMode -incremental false” to eliminate timing differences between optDesign -
postRoute (when timed with incremental TQRC) vs. timeDesign -postRoute (with a full re-
extraction).
Conclusion
While this problem is not widespread, some designs have shows a problematic level of timing
miscorrelation between incremental and full TQRC.
COPYRIGHT © 2013, CADENCE DESIGN SYSTEMS, INC. PAGE 9
Designs which appear most susceptible to this condition are:
• Based on standard cells libraries with weak drivers available for use
• Are relatively easy to achieve timing closure (since many weak driver cells can be used
on near-critical paths unless otherwise restricted)
• Have a small amount of timing corrections needed (and therefore allow for repeated calls
to incremental rather than full TQRC extraction)
COPYRIGHT © 2013, CADENCE DESIGN SYSTEMS, INC. PAGE 10
